entries.notes: UMBRELLA entry covering the entire Turbo genus reef cleanup-crew trade: Turbo fluctuosus (Mexican Turbo), Turbo brunneus (Indo-Pacific), Turbo bruneus (Banded Turbo), Turbo castanea (Chestnut Turbo, Caribbean), Turbo petholatus (Tapestry Turbo, Indo-Pacific). Large coldwater-tolerant Mexican Turbo prefer 20-24°C; warmer Caribbean/Indo-Pacific congeners prefer typical reef 23-27°C — note this dimorphism in careNotes. Powerful algae grazers; can topple frags. Frequent escape artists.

alsoKnownAs: Astrea Snail (alternate spelling), Lithopoma tectum (current accepted taxonomy — Astraea tecta is older), Star Astraea, Conehead Snail, West Indian Star Snail. Caribbean cleanup-crew workhorse. Smaller than Turbo, less destructive. Common 'gotcha': cannot right themselves if knocked upside-down — they slowly die unless aquarist intervenes. Flag in careNotes.

UMBRELLA entry for the Trochus genus: Trochus radiatus, Trochus histrio, Trochus niloticus (Banded Trochus / Top Shell). Pyramid-shaped algae grazers — RIGHT THEMSELVES when toppled (unlike Astraea — major advantage). Captive-bred lines available from ORA and others. Reef-safe workhorse cleanup.

UMBRELLA entry for the Cerithium genus: Cerithium litteratum (Florida Cerith), Cerithium nodulosum (Pacific Cerith), Cerithium spp. Small (~2-3 cm) sand-bed cleaner and detritivore. Burrows during the day, active at night. Lays distinctive 'spaghetti' egg strings on glass. Workhorse reef cleanup-crew. Reef safe.

UMBRELLA entry covering the Nassarius reef-detritivore trade: Nassarius vibex (Caribbean), Nassarius distortus (Indo-Pacific), Nassarius spp. Sand-bed burrower that springs out at feeding time to scavenge protein. Long siphon visible above substrate. The standard sand-bed cleanup mollusc for reef tanks. Reef safe.

alsoKnownAs: Striped Engina, Bumble Bee Snail. PREDATORY — eats vermetid snails, small worms, and sometimes harasses other snails. Distinct from the FW Bumblebee Snail (Anentome helena / Clea helena Assassin Snail) — flag the common-name collision in careNotes. Not strictly reef-safe in all senses (predates other snails) but coral-safe.

UMBRELLA entry covering the Cypraeidae trade: Monetaria moneta (Money Cowrie), Cypraea tigris (Tiger Cowrie — much larger, ~10 cm), Cypraea annulus (Ring Cowrie), Cypraea spp. Small cowries are reef-safe algae grazers; large cowries (Tiger) outgrow nano tanks and may pick at corals — flag the size-related caveat. Decorate themselves with shell fragments. The brilliant mantle covers and uncovers the shell.

UMBRELLA entry covering the Strombus reef-sandbed-cleaner trade: Strombus alatus (Florida Fighting Conch — most common), Strombus luhuanus (Strawberry Conch — Indo-Pacific), Strombus gigas (Queen Conch — too large for home tanks, drop). Reef-safe sand-bed cleaner. Deep sand bed required (5+ cm). Active forager. Eye-stalks on tentacles characteristic.

UMBRELLA entry covering the Tridacna giant-clam trade group: Tridacna maxima (Maxima — high light, common ornamental), Tridacna crocea (Crocea — highest light, smallest), Tridacna derasa (Derasa — lower light tolerant, larger), Tridacna squamosa (Squamosa — scalloped shell, intermediate). Care differs in lighting demand (Maxima/Crocea high — needs reef-grade lighting; Derasa/Squamosa moderate-to-high) and adult size (Derasa to 60 cm; Crocea max ~15 cm). All require: stable calcium 400+ ppm, magnesium 1300+ ppm, alkalinity 8-12 dKH, phytoplankton supplementation when small, and copper-free system. CITES Appendix II listed — legal trade requires permits. Color morphs (Squamigera/Gold/Teardrop/Ultra) covered. Notes flag the lighting+size differences clearly so end-users can choose.

alsoKnownAs: Electric Flame Scallop, Electric Scallop, Ctenoides scaber (current accepted name; Lima scabra is older), Flame-edge Bivalve. Filter-feeding bivalve with bioluminescent-looking flashing mantle (actually light-reflection, not bioluminescence). Specialist — requires phytoplankton supplementation, mature tank, almost always slowly starves in standard reef systems. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only with the high-mortality reason.

alsoKnownAs: Bimac Octopus, California Two-Spot Octopus, Pacific Two-Spot. Most popular pet octopus in the hobby. California Pacific coastal — coldwater (16-22°C), distinct from tropical reef setups. Highly intelligent, short-lived (~12-18 months). Will eat any tank-mate; species-only tank with cycled escape-proof setup required. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only. Captive-bred lines from a few breeders. Distinct from tropical dwarf octopus (separate entry).

UMBRELLA entry: Octopus joubini (Atlantic Pygmy), Octopus mercatoris (Caribbean Pygmy — frequently sold under joubini name). Tropical (24-27°C). Very small (~5-8 cm mantle). Short-lived (~6-12 months). Same species-only-tank + escape-proof requirements as bimaculoides. Distinct from blue-ring (Hapalochlaena) — DEADLY VENOM, NEVER for the hobby (intentionally not included in this slice). Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only.

alsoKnownAs: Stumpy-Spined Cuttlefish, Banded Cuttlefish, Pygmy Cuttlefish. The most successful pet cuttlefish in the hobby. Small (~7 cm mantle), short-lived (~6-12 months). Captive-bred lines available. Eats live amphipods/mysis as juveniles, transitions to frozen shrimp. Species-only tank. Set hobbyistAdvisory: specialist-only.

UMBRELLA entry: Aplysia californica (California Sea Hare — coldwater Pacific), Bursatella leachii (Ragged Sea Hare — tropical), Dolabella auricularia (Wedge Sea Hare — Indo-Pacific, often sold for hair-algae control). Sold primarily as algae-control specialists for hair-algae outbreaks. INK RELEASE when stressed — flag the toxic-ink caveat (can kill tank with skimmer running). Short-lived (~1 yr). Will starve once they've eaten all the algae.

alsoKnownAs: Aiptasia-eating Nudibranch, Berghia verrucicornis (older taxonomy — current accepted is Berghia stephanieae), Aeolid Nudibranch. Obligate Aiptasia anemone predator — sold specifically for biological pest-anemone control. Will eventually starve once Aiptasia is eliminated unless re-released. Reef safe to corals/non-Aiptasia.

alsoKnownAs: Lettuce Nudibranch, Crowned Elysia, Solar-Powered Sea Slug. Photosynthetic sea slug — sequesters chloroplasts from green algae (kleptoplasty) and uses them for energy. Eats Bryopsis and hair algae. Reef safe but specialist — needs algae diet to maintain chloroplast load. Distinct from sea hares (Aplysia).
